summaries.headline: Early Readers Behind in First Grade Stay Behind
summaries.summary: First graders who struggle with reading have an 88 percent chance of remaining behind by fourth grade, according to longitudinal research highlighting the urgency of early literacy intervention. Traditional approaches that treat third grade as the finish line for learning to read leave too many students without the phonics skills needed for increasingly complex upper-grade texts, where vocabulary becomes longer and more morphologically demanding. The persistence of reading gaps suggests schools need both rigorous instruction and efficient acceleration strategies rather than simplified content for struggling readers.

summaries.headline: Illinois Lawmakers Push $3.9 Billion School Funding Increase
summaries.summary: State legislators backed by the Chicago Teachers Union introduced bills requiring Illinois to fully fund its evidence-based school formula by 2027, a goal that would cost an additional $3.9 billion annually. The state currently increases K-12 funding by roughly $350 million per year but would need to boost that to over $1 billion annually to meet the deadline lawmakers set nearly a decade ago. The push comes as districts face mounting financial pressures and supporters frame increased state investment as essential while federal education funding faces potential cuts under the Trump administration.

summaries.headline: Utah's Data Science Pilot Shows Mixed Results
summaries.summary: Utah launched a pilot program three years ago with 16 high school math teachers to introduce data science courses, splitting a $20,000 state grant to help students analyze real-world data and potentially connect with the state's growing tech sector. The initiative reflects a broader national trend, with 14 states now offering data science pilots or teacher training and seven more developing formal standards for the subject. The expansion highlights an ongoing tension between preparing students for immediate workforce opportunities in data-driven fields versus maintaining emphasis on traditional math foundations like algebra and calculus that remain essential for selective college admissions and STEM careers.

summaries.headline: Michigan Governor Proposes $625 Million Literacy Investment
summaries.summary: Gov. Gretchen Whitmer will unveil a $625 million literacy plan this week targeting Michigan's reading crisis, where just 38.9% of third graders scored proficient on state tests last year — the lowest performance in the exam's 11-year history. The investment would expand free preschool access regardless of income and increase teacher training in science of reading methods, as Michigan fourth graders also trail the national average by 6 percentage points on federal assessments. The proposal signals recognition that Michigan's higher-than-average spending hasn't translated to results, while other states investing in early literacy have seen improvements.

summaries.headline: Literacy Instruction Shifts From Buzzwords to Implementation
summaries.summary: Literacy educators are moving beyond surface-level adoption of Science of Reading terminology toward comprehensive, research-backed practices that integrate multiple components of reading instruction. Schools are abandoning isolated phonemic awareness drills and "wait and see" intervention approaches in favor of connecting sounds to print from the start and implementing universal screening with immediate action for at-risk readers. The shift reflects growing recognition that effective literacy instruction requires systemic implementation of evidence-based practices rather than piecemeal adoption of popular methodologies.

summaries.headline: Superintendent Urges Tailored Approach to Combat Chronic Absenteeism
summaries.summary: District leaders need individualized strategies to address chronic absenteeism rather than universal solutions, according to Fox Chapel Area Superintendent Mary Catherine Reljac. National data shows about 23% of K-12 students were chronically absent in 2024-25, missing 10% or more of school days despite slight recent improvements from post-pandemic highs. Reljac's emphasis on proactive, sensitive family communication and data-driven student support suggests successful attendance interventions require understanding the specific barriers each student faces.

summaries.headline: Delaware School Funding Reveals Four-Bucket Complexity
summaries.summary: Delaware's public K-12 system operates on more than $2 billion annually from four distinct funding streams, each with different rules and purposes. The state's operating budget provides the largest share at $1.8 billion (29% of all state spending), while capital projects receive $238.8 million through bond funding, local property taxes contribute $500-550 million, and federal aid adds $250-300 million. The fragmented structure explains why education budget debates focus not just on total spending but on which funding bucket pays for what, creating constraints that district leaders must navigate when planning programs and infrastructure.

summaries.headline: Chicago School Board Ends CEO Search Firm Contract
summaries.summary: The Chicago Board of Education terminated its contract with Alma Advisory Group, the search firm helping find a new permanent CEO to replace Pedro Martinez, who was fired in June. Six of the board's 10 elected members claim mayoral appointees "sabotaged" the search process and now want interim CEO Macquline King to remain until a new fully elected board takes office in January. The breakdown adds another layer of dysfunction to a district that has operated without permanent leadership for eight months, with political tensions between elected and appointed board members now spilling into the CEO selection process.

summaries.headline: NYC School Board Member Uses Slur, Faces Calls to Apologize
summaries.summary: Joe Borelli, a member of New York City's Panel for Educational Policy, used the r-word in a social media post criticizing a congressman's Super Bowl photo, drawing immediate backlash from the school board chair and parent leaders. Borelli, appointed by former Mayor Eric Adams in his final weeks, oversees a district where more than 200,000 students — over one in five — have disabilities. The incident highlights tensions around Adams' last-minute board appointments, which will prevent incoming Mayor Zohran Mamdani from appointing a board majority until June.

summaries.headline: Illinois Counties Ask Voters About Trump Scholarship Program
summaries.summary: More than two dozen Illinois counties and townships will include a nonbinding ballot question in next month's primary asking voters to support a Trump-backed federal tax credit scholarship program that requires state opt-in. The Illinois Policy Institute's advocacy arm crafted the ballot language, which describes using "privately-donated funds" for tutoring and test preparation but omits that donors receive dollar-for-dollar federal tax credits up to $1,700 for contributions to scholarship-granting nonprofits. The unusual campaign targeting a strongly Democratic state aims to pressure Gov. JB Pritzker into joining the program, though federal officials haven't clarified key implementation details that could shape how families and schools actually use the funds.

summaries.headline: LEGO Education Launches Hands-On Computer Science Curriculum
summaries.summary: LEGO Education will begin shipping a new K-8 computer science and AI curriculum in April 2026, designed to move students away from isolated screen-based coding activities toward collaborative, physical learning experiences. The curriculum includes stackable kits for three grade bands, each containing LEGO bricks with integrated motors and sensors plus 30 inquiry-based lessons that can supplement existing programs or serve as standalone computer science instruction. The approach addresses growing concerns that traditional "learn to code" initiatives often reduce programming to repetitive, disconnected exercises that fail to engage students' natural curiosity and collaborative instincts.

summaries.headline: Oregon Lawmakers Eye School Funding Overhaul Amid Cuts
summaries.summary: Oregon legislators are considering a complete restructuring of the state's school funding formula as districts across the state implement budget reductions. The current funding model has left many districts struggling to maintain programs and staffing levels, prompting calls for a more equitable and sustainable approach to distributing state education dollars. The timing suggests lawmakers recognize that incremental fixes may no longer suffice as economic pressures force districts into difficult decisions about core educational services.

summaries.headline: Illinois Lawmakers Push Faster School Funding Increases
summaries.summary: Chicago-area legislators are introducing bills to accelerate Illinois school funding increases, targeting both mandated categorical programs and evidence-based funding formulas. State Rep. Will Davis acknowledged the state needs $550 million to $1 billion more annually to reach full funding, despite years of consecutive increases. The push comes as lawmakers frame schools as essential community hubs while facing potential federal funding uncertainty and ongoing state budget constraints.

summaries.headline: Multilingual Students Need Context, Not Just Phonics
summaries.summary: Veteran North Carolina educator Terri Ashchi argues that multilingual students often master decoding skills but struggle with comprehension because traditional literacy instruction ignores their cultural backgrounds and lived experiences. Her students frequently tell her "I can read, but I don't know what it means," highlighting the gap between technical reading ability and meaningful understanding when materials lack cultural relevance. This disconnect suggests that effective literacy instruction for the nation's growing multilingual population requires teaching materials and methods that connect to students' identities and prior knowledge rather than relying solely on phonics-heavy approaches.

summaries.headline: Denver Arts School Expansion Promise Falls Short
summaries.summary: Denver School of the Arts promised to expand from 1,100 to 1,700 students and diversify its overwhelmingly white, affluent student body through a $30 million property purchase in 2021, including plans to reduce reliance on auditions that favor students with private training. The school has made modest demographic gains — students of color increased from 31% to 38% and low-income students from 11% to 14% — but enrollment has actually shrunk rather than grown, and the audition-based admissions system remains unchanged. The gap between DSA's demographics and the broader Denver district, where most students are of color and from low-income families, illustrates how specialized schools can struggle to balance excellence with equity even when expansion resources are available.

summaries.headline: House Panel Examines Science of Reading Implementation
summaries.summary: House appropriators heard testimony on evidence-based literacy instruction, with witnesses from the University of Florida, Alabama's state reading initiative, and Huntsville City Schools presenting on phonics-focused approaches. Lawmakers highlighted the critical window for reading proficiency, with one representative noting that students who don't reach grade level by third grade "just going to get further and further behind," while another pointed to Baltimore schools where a historically successful high school now shows 0% reading proficiency. The hearing signals potential federal funding priorities as Congress weighs how to support districts transitioning to science of reading methods amid ongoing concerns about teacher preparation and curriculum quality.

summaries.headline: Illinois Lawmakers Target Unfulfilled School Funding Promises
summaries.summary: Illinois lawmakers and educators introduced legislation requiring the state to fully implement funding commitments made in a 2017 school finance law that supporters say remains unfulfilled. The proposed bill would mandate complete state compliance with the earlier funding formula designed to boost resources for the lowest-funded districts, while also requiring full funding for categorical programs covering special education and transportation. The measure signals growing pressure on state governments to follow through on equity-focused funding reforms as districts continue reporting significant budget shortfalls despite previous legislative promises.

summaries.headline: Marsabit County Feeds 24,000 Students During Drought
summaries.summary: Marsabit County in Kenya is serving nutritionally fortified porridge to 24,000 early childhood learners across 374 centers as drought devastates local families. Chief Officer Education Qabale Adhi Bulbul said the feeding program aims to boost attendance and retention while supporting cognitive development during a critical learning period. The initiative demonstrates how counties can use nutrition interventions to maintain educational access when economic shocks threaten to pull young children out of school.

summaries.headline: Cloud Security Gaps Leave Districts Exposed Despite Basic Protections
summaries.summary: K-12 districts are securing cloud platforms like Google Workspace and Microsoft 365 with outdated approaches designed for on-premises systems, according to cybersecurity experts. Most districts rely entirely on network firewalls and vendor-provided admin tools that weren't built to adequately protect cloud-based data and user activities. The shift to cloud-centric operations has fundamentally changed what constitutes minimum viable cybersecurity, yet many districts haven't adapted their security strategies to match their digital infrastructure.

summaries.headline: Illinois Faces $3.3B School Funding Gap
summaries.summary: Illinois lawmakers are advancing legislation to address a $3.3 billion funding shortfall that affects nearly 3.3 million students across underfunded districts statewide. State representatives are pushing bills HB5409 and SB3701 to fix "mandated categoricals" — programs like special education services and student transportation that the state requires but fails to fund adequately. The push comes as legislators approach a 2017-established deadline to ensure every school receives adequate funding, highlighting the gap between state mandates and financial support for essential student services.

synthesis.topic_title: State Funding Formulas Under Fire as Districts Turn to Local Revenue
synthesis.whats_happening: State lawmakers across multiple jurisdictions are grappling with fundamental questions about school funding adequacy and distribution methods. Districts increasingly rely on local levies to bridge gaps left by state funding systems that stakeholders describe as broken or constitutionally inadequate.
synthesis.whats_driving: Post-pandemic fiscal pressures have intensified longstanding tensions between state funding obligations and local capacity to supplement education budgets. Constitutional mandates for adequate funding collide with legislative reluctance to increase state appropriations, pushing more burden onto property taxpayers.

- State funding formulas face reform pressure due to structural inadequacies in current allocation methods
- Local property taxpayers bear increasing burden as districts turn to levies for essential operations
- Constitutional funding mandates create legal tension with legislative appropriation decisions

When I was growing up, I knew I wanted to become a teacher. But by my late teens, I realized I didn’t like being in school all day, so why would I spend my adult life working in one?

I don’t know what I would have done if I had walked down that career path. What I know now, after watching and writing about education for many years in Wyoming, is that I admire the teaching profession.

But if I was a teacher in this state today, or studying to be one, I’d look at how legislators fund public schools and question how serious Wyoming lawmakers are about improving, or even maintaining, our educational system.

Wyoming has lost every lawsuit over school funding since the 1980s. In a landmark 1995 decision, the state Supreme Court declared the entire K-12 funding system unconstitutional and established a “basket of educational goods and services” that forced the state to calculate the actual cost of education, rather than appropriate the amount of money legislators deemed available.

Laramie County District Court Judge Peter Froelicher ruled last February that the state is again unconstitutionally underfunding education. The state is failing to adequately pay teachers and staff, properly adjust for inflation and provide sufficient funding for mental health counselors, school safety resource officers, nutritional programs and computers for students.

Beginning in 2005, the state Supreme Court ordered the Legislature to conduct a “recalibration” of its school funding model every five years. Froelicher wrote that the iteration of that cycle set to begin in summer 2025 was “an excellent time” to fix these problems.

The Select Committee on School Finance Recalibration unanimously approved a $1.8 billion bill ahead of the budget session that begins Monday. On Jan. 23, when it began its final meeting, the committee had cut the state consultant’s evidence-based funding recommendation for a $100 million increase to only about $12 million.

That’s not unusual, given that lawmakers often toss the private consultant’s findings aside and do whatever they please. But on the heels of Froelicher’s meticulously detailed 186-page ruling, it seemed purposely defiant, so educators and others told the committee it wasn’t doing its job.

Several amendments added $30 million, but the Wyoming Education Association — which sued the state, leading to the judge’s order — isn’t buying the committee’s claim that it reasonably compromised on the issues. Neither am I.

The bill initially cut 600 core-subject teaching positions. The committee eventually reduced that cut to about 300. But the final version of the draft bill passed by lawmakers increases student-teacher ratios for middle and high schools from 21:1 to 25:1.

More students equals more work for teachers, especially with fewer of them in the classrooms.

The average salary paid to teachers by districts was $65,265 for the 2024-25 school year. The recalibration bill would raise the average teacher salary to $70,560.

But the state’s consultant heard testimony from education experts that said a livable starting salary for Wyoming teachers would be $70,000.

Legislators have inconsistently approved “external cost adjustments,” or temporary funding increases, in recent years. In fact, the Legislature didn’t even consider an “external cost adjustment” last year until a day after Froelicher’s ruling.

The recalibration bill would allow for such an adjustment every two years. But the Wyoming Education Association called it a two-year salary freeze with no guarantees for future adjustments. If future Legislatures fail to provide for a full and ongoing external cost adjustment after two years, the result would be a four-year salary freeze, a significant loss of purchasing power for teachers and staff.

The state is appealing Froelicher’s ruling, so the Legislature bought itself more time to recalibrate during the 2026 interim. “We just don’t have a silver bullet to fix everything within one fell swoop, and it’s going to take time to hear all the testimony,” claimed the committee’s co-chair, Rep. Scott Heiner, R-Green River.

But Albany County School District 1’s business manager, Trystin Green, shot Heiner’s excuse down stone cold.

“Another thing that’s a little bit hard for us to see is how we can pass a gun law in three months and allow guns in school for kids,” said Green, “but we need 24 months to determine if we need extra counselors or (school resource officers) or extra support staff to address our mental health issues?”

There’s plenty of reasons to agree with the WEA’s conclusion about recalibration thus far: “The committee offered no real fixes and instead dangled scraps from the table.”

Those scraps are teacher pay raises that seem far less significant than when they were first dangled last summer. And they don’t begin to make up for not addressing critical school funding shortages identified by the judge that Wyoming cannot afford to put on the back burner.

Whatever your stake is in public education, please let your legislators know that Wyoming needs to fully fund our schools. If lawmakers don’t listen, they will inevitably find themselves back in court, spending yet more money on lawyers instead of education.

**The Drake’s Take** is a weekly column by veteran Wyoming journalist Kerry Drake, and produced by [WyoFile.com](http://wyofile.com/), a nonprofit news organization focused on Wyoming people, places and policy.

Approving a public school funding model tops the educational to-do list of legislators convening Monday in Cheyenne. The so-called recalibration bill, K-12 public school finance, will impact how teachers are paid, how big class sizes are and how schools pay for things like mental health counselors and school resource officers.

Lawmakers also will consider bills related to statewide literacy instruction, cellphones in schools and student discipline. Draft bills also deal with suicide prevention, homeschooled students and eligibility for Wyoming’s Hathaway college scholarships.

With a breakneck pace expected during the Wyoming Legislature’s 20-day budget session, here are school-related bills to keep an eye on.

### School funding

Every five years, the Legislature is tasked with assessing and updating the state’s constitutionally mandated school funding model. The model influences everything from how many students are in a classroom to how much a superintendent is paid to what’s served for lunch in school cafeterias.

The current process of recalibration, as it is known, has been unfolding in a series of meetings, studies and assessments since last summer. It comes with extra gravity following a 2025 court decision that Wyoming has been failing to properly fund its public schools.

Working with consultants, the Legislature’s Select Committee on School Finance Recalibration has drafted and refined a bill dictating how it will pay for public schools. The process has been scrutinized by educators and others who say Wyoming has failed to keep up with teacher salaries, leading to a staff shortages and erosion of Wyoming’s historically stellar public school reputation.

During the most recent meeting in January, educators, parents and others gave hours of testimony to the committee, praising pay raises for teachers, but slamming the ways in which it would raise classroom sizes, eliminate hundreds of full-time teacher roles, force districts to join the state insurance pool and fail to address issues like school nutrition and mental health counseling as mandated by the 2025 court order.

The committee made several amendments to the draft bill, modifying it to make smaller increases to class sizes, adjusting the minimum number of teachers in small schools and inserting a placeholder for mental health, nurses, counselors and related support funding. Despite that, superintendents have argued that the draft funding model significantly reduces local control and remains problematic.

“As the Wyoming Legislative Budget Session begins Monday, the Recalibration Bill … deserves close attention from communities across the state,” Sheridan County School District 1 Superintendent Jeff Jones wrote in an opinion piece submitted to WyoFile. “This version of the bill misses the mark in three fundamental ways: It seriously undervalues funding for teacher salaries, significantly reduces local control, and regrettably increases class sizes in our schools.”

### Literacy

Literacy instruction is emerging as a nationwide issue with the downturn of reading scores in recent years. While Wyoming continues to rank comparatively high in national testing, literacy challenges still appear here.

In 2024, 36% of the state’s fourth-graders and 29% of eighth-graders performed at or above the proficient level in reading on national standardized NAEP tests, lower than the previous five years.

Some 32% of Wyoming fourth-graders performed below basic levels, which was a slight increase from 29% in 2022. For eighth-graders, 30% scored below basic levels in 2024, up one percentage point from 2022.

News reports and studies have shifted how the literacy field views reading instruction — a method of teaching reading that was prevalent for many years was widely debunked by the 2022 podcast “Sold a Story.” At least 26 states have passed laws about how schools teach reading since it began to air.

Wyoming could be next. Senate File 59, “K-12 language and literacy,” aims to ensure that every K-12 Wyoming student develops strong language and literacy skills and that struggling readers do not fall through the cracks.

The legislation resulted largely from the work of a literacy subcommittee with input from stakeholders working on a new statewide literacy initiative — including a group of parents and educators focused on better identification and treatment of conditions like dyslexia. The bill would establish a system of instruction, intervention and professional development that is evidence-based to provide teachers, families and students with comprehensive and effective tools for teaching reading and addressing deficiencies.

### Cellphones and discipline

Last year, Sen. Wendy Schuler, an Evanston Republican, brought a bill requiring Wyoming school districts to adopt policies prohibiting cellphone use in classrooms. Schuler, a retired teacher, said she was motivated by a common teacher complaint that policing phone use has become a classroom nightmare.

The bill failed to pass, with lawmakers expressing concerns such as government overreach. But Schuler and several other lawmakers have brought a new version for consideration in 2026. Senate File 35, “School district-cell phone and smart watch policies,” requires districts to adopt policies governing students’ possession and use of cellphones and smartwatches in schools.

The bill comes as 30 U.S. states have passed laws or guidelines banning or restricting student cellphone use in schools. Several Wyoming districts also have individual policies. Advocates say keeping devices out of classrooms curbs distractions, improves mental health and helps focus and performance.

Another growing classroom concern is behavioral issues, which have reportedly spiked since the COVID-19 pandemic, and the appropriate discipline to address them. House Bill 65, “K-12 public school discipline,” would require school boards to establish and adopt rules for student discipline. The bill would clarify requirements for parental notice, student confidentiality, enforcement and reporting. It would also repeal immunity for the use of corporal discipline, meaning that educators would no longer have de-facto legal protection for hitting students.

### Other bills in play

House Bill 23, “Participation in 6-12 activities,” would allow homeschooled students to participate in extracurricular activities in public schools such as sports. House Bill 40, “Suicide prevention,” would require districts to provide suicide prevention education to students. House Bill 57, “Hathaway private post secondary institution scholarships,” would extend the use of Wyoming’s Hathaway scholarships to private colleges or institutions. The scholarship is currently limited to students attending the University of Wyoming or Wyoming’s community colleges. And House Bill 74, “Public schools to provide feminine hygiene products,” would require school districts to provide feminine hygiene products in restrooms.

![](https://www.pressherald.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/4/2026/02/43356096_20260107_Session_8107.jpg?w=1200)Senate President Mattie Daughtry, D-Brunswick, center, presides on on the first day of second regular session of the 132nd Maine Legislature Wednesday January 7, 2026 in Maine State House in Augusta. (Joe Phelan/Staff Photographer)

Maine’s formula for distributing state funding to local school districts — which lawmakers, school leaders and researchers have described as inequitable for more than a decade — could be getting an update.

Members of the Legislature’s Education and Cultural Affairs Committee voted Wednesday to introduce a bill incorporating major changes to the funding formula recommended by the nonpartisan [Maine Educational Policy Research Institute](https://mepri.maine.edu/), which it directed to study the issue in 2024.

The current formula is a complicated puzzle that uses enrollment numbers, among a variety of other factors, to assess a district’s needs, and calculates a district’s ability to pay those costs based on property tax valuations to figure out how much the state will cover. Subsidies range from less than 10% of a district’s costs to more than 80%.

The goals of the study, institute co-director Amy Johnson said, were to ensure that the model accurately reflects the costs of schooling today, and to improve fairness in the distribution of the state subsidy. The institute [offered four recommendations](https://bpb-us-w2.wpmucdn.com/wpsites.maine.edu/dist/e/97/files/2026/02/LD318_FinalReport_with_Appendices.pdf):

- **Align regional adjustments with cost of living, minimum salaries**

The current funding formula accounts for regional differences in salary to provide adequate staffing resources to districts in both high- and low-cost areas in Maine. Johnson said the labor market data used to calculate those regional adjustments has not been updated since the formula was created in 2005.

The institute recommends basing the adjustments on annually updated cost-of-living data, and fixing the adjustments to align with minimum salaries (what the state now uses for its teacher salary matrix), rather than averages.

Advertisement

- **Factor community poverty rates into local “ability to contribute” calculations**

Maine Educational Policy Research Institute proposes a 90/10 model for calculating how much of their costs districts can afford to cover with local funding. That means 90% of the expectation would be based on property taxes (the current system) and 10% would be based on the economically disadvantaged student rate. Researchers found that rate to be the best proxy for the poverty level in a community.

This adjustment is designed to tackle the phenomenon of communities that have high property values but do not have the local income levels to match.

- **Update parts of the formula related to transportation, tech and instructional staff to reflect modern educational needs**

This recommendation cleans up several parts of the formula that Johnson said are outdated.

- **Reform the special education component of the formula**

Johnson said [special education is the area of the model](https://www.pressherald.com/2025/03/26/researchers-recommend-changes-to-special-ed-funding-under-state-formula-review/) that is “the most under stress.” Because of a step in the formula that bases state funding on past spending, the current formula disproportionately privileges wealthier districts.

But before changing the formula, the institute proposes shifting special education to a regional model, wherein districts would collaborate on providing special ed services. Researchers are planning a forthcoming special education-specific report.

The committee voted unanimously Wednesday to initiate a committee bill, which means it is directing the Legislative Information Office to draft a bill based on the institute’s recommendations. A public hearing has not yet been set.

Advertisement

Researchers laid out broad issues with the formula in a 200-page report in July, then [presented the formal recommendations](https://bpb-us-w2.wpmucdn.com/wpsites.maine.edu/dist/e/97/files/2026/02/EPS-Study-2026-LD318_Slides_Handouts.pdf) to lawmakers last week.

This isn’t the first time the Legislature has attempted to reform the model. Most recently, lawmakers commissioned a [comprehensive review in 2013](https://www.pressherald.com/2013/12/11/education_committee_hopes_to_introduce_school_funding_reform_bill_/), but did not adopt any of the changes proposed.

The forthcoming bill is being developed as districts across Maine are beginning their budgeting process for the next fiscal year. At a [public budget forum Tuesday night](https://www.pressherald.com/2026/02/10/four-takeaways-from-portlands-school-budget-forum/), Portland’s finance director said the district expects to get $4 million less from the state in the coming year through the formula, thanks to declines in enrollment and increases in property valuation.

“There could not be a clearer indicator that this funding formula is not working the way that it was intended, and we need rapid changes to that,” school board Chair Sarah Lentz said Tuesday. She said the board planned to take that message to Augusta.

MOULTON — A potential state funding slowdown expected in the next few years could shape the future of Lawrence County Schools, Superintendent Jon Bret Smith told the Board of Education.

In his superintendent’s report last week, he said that current state finances look strong. But a glance into the near future has made him hit the brakes when considering what’s possible.

“We worry about the future so we can kind of forecast what’s coming down the road and FY (fiscal year) '27 looks fairly strong; FY '28 does not,” Smith said. “There’s going to be an economic slowdown.”

Smith said they aren’t entering an economic slump, it just looks like funding from the state could slow down from the substantial growth they’ve seen over the past few years.

“ We think that we’re not going to necessarily decline but instead of like a 6% growth, there’s only a 2% growth,” Smith said, “Which sustains what we have, but it doesn’t add anything to that.”

He said it’s nothing to be immediately concerned with, but as the possible slowdown approaches, it will affect more and more decisions.

“One of the biggest ones that I’m concerned about is the advancement and technology funds,” he said. “For years, we have used those to pay our insurance, and to pay our SROs (school resource officers). If you couple the SROs and the insurance together, that’s more than a million dollars.  If the current budget holds true, I think most of that money will be depleted this legislative cycle.”

Also as part of his superintendent report, Smith said the school system has made plans to hire another SRO.

“ I’m very proud to recommend an update to the SRO contract. Our original budget, the current contract does not reflect the budget number because we could not find enough SROs,” he said. “But our local Sheriff’s Office has found another individual that’s wanting to be trained. This would put us much closer to the overall goal of having an SRO on every school campus.”

He also mentioned the system’s Legal Explorer Program, which starts at the end of February.

“Jerome Thompson and the local bar association have been very adamant that in the next five years, there will be a considerable number of local attorneys that are retiring,” he said. “So they’re trying to cultivate some interest in some of our high school students.”

Since the return from Christmas break, flu season has been in full swing. Smith said the board’s been keeping a close eye on it, hoping it doesn’t affect daily operations.

“It seemed like right around Christmas or as we returned from Christmas, that the flu numbers had spiked significantly. We were really concerned that it may affect our schools,” Smith said. “The severity of this year’s flu cases were much worse than in years past. However, it does seem like the numbers have decreased, but the severity has not.”

District 5 board member Kenny Hood expressed interest in trying to speed up the repairs at the East Lawrence High football field, which has had drainage issues over at least the past five years.

“ I’d like to see this board put in place something to make sure that the incoming football coach knows that this board is actively working to make sure that we bring this football field up to par,” Hood said.

Smith responded by saying the bid hasn’t been put out yet and they’re still working with an architect on a permanent plan. He said the board in December approved having civil engineers come up with a plan.

“So instead of backfilling what we have in the past or making temporary fixes, it’s going to be a significant cost, but it also is going to be a plan that I think will solve all of our issues,” Smith said.

Iowa Sen. **Tom Townsend**, D-Dubuque, last week in a statement slammed Senate File 2201, which would further restrict the amount of funding for public school districts.

The bill would cap the annual increase in funding for public schools at 1.75%. The current U.S. inflation rate is 2.7%. The senator reasoned that this would result in further cuts to Iowa’s K-12 education amidst an already stressed system, as several school districts across the state have reported closures and consolidations in recent years.

“Schools will be forced to cut teacher positions,” Townsend said. “Districts will cut programs. We’ll see school consolidations and larger class sizes — some districts have even talked about combining entire grade levels.

“At these proposed funding levels, some school districts won’t be able to meet the new minimum teacher salary requirements the Legislature passed a couple of years ago,” he said.

Dubuque Community School District is already feeling the pressure of tight budgets, having closed two elementary schools in the last four years.

The bill passed through the Senate Committee on Education on Wednesday and now moves to the full chamber for debate.

### Chesney blasts Illinois governor for declining population

Illinois Sen. **Andrew Chesney**, R-Freeport, in a recent statement, addressed Illinois’ high outmigration rate and called for the governor to focus on bettering the quality of life for state residents.

In the statement, the senator blamed high taxes, excess government spending, high energy costs, poor public safety and rising pension debt as various factors that force Illinois families elsewhere. He also blamed the governor for prioritizing interviews with national media outlets over local and state newspapers.

“People do not abandon their home state lightly,” Chesney said. “They leave when government takes more and delivers less.”

He cited data from the U.S. Census Bureau suggesting that 83,000 Illinoisans left the state, but didn’t specify the time frame. A recent report from the office of Gov. **JB Pritzker** disputes those numbers, stating that Illinois gained over 16,000 residents from July 2024 to July 2025.

“The state remains focused on strengthening the factors that help people choose to stay and build their lives here,” the governor said in a statement.

Data collected from moving companies and published by the Illinois Policy Institute indicates that although the state has lost over 400,000 residents since 2020, the rate at which people are leaving slowed in 2025. However, Illinois still ranks near the top of all states in terms of outmigration.

The organization, which is focused on tax reform, has called for lower taxes, increased employment opportunities and more affordable housing to address the issue.

### Marklein recaps listening sessions in 17th Senate District

Wisconsin Sen. **Howard Marklein**, R-Spring Green, last week released a statement recapping his various listening sessions held in the winter throughout the 17th senate district, including a visit to Cuba City.

Among several issues brought to the legislator’s attention by his constituents, school funding, especially regarding rural school districts, was a chief concern. Marklein expressed his desire to continue fighting for rural schools, but blamed school districts’ struggles on declining enrollment throughout the state.

“Declining enrollment has caused a shrinking revenue limit, meaning the size of (schools’) budgets are decreasing regardless of the amount of school funding,” he said.

Another hot topic throughout Southwest Wisconsin is that of approving developments relating to renewable energy or artificial intelligence. The senator referenced Senate Bill 3, a bill authored himself, as a solution that would give local governments more of a say in decisions regarding those developments.

Marklein also emphasized property taxes as a chief issue that concerned both him and his constituents, and cited two reasons for recent tax jumps in the statement.

“I experienced a similar increase this year,” he said. “Rising property taxes were primarily driven by two factors: school districts going to referendum due to declining enrollment and the governor’s 400-year-veto.”

The senator went on to express his desire to repeal the veto, which currently requires that school districts increase their funding by $325 annually until the year 2425.

CHEYENNE — A bill to fund Wyoming’s K-12 public-school system failed introduction in the House of Representatives on the opening day of the Wyoming Legislature’s budget session — the legislative equivalent of death.

The Legislature is constitutionally required to fund its public schools and is tasked every five years with the so-called “recalibration” process. The exhaustive task involves assessing the state’s school funding model and making necessary updates; it impacts everything from teacher pay to class sizes to school nutrition in all 48 districts.

The House version of the bill, House Bill 110, “K-12 Public School Finance,” was unpopular among Wyoming educators and school administrators.

A special committee of lawmakers drafted the bill over several months with the help of consultants. Though many educators praised a teacher pay raise, critics blasted elements of it that would raise classroom sizes, mandate school districts take part in the state health insurance program and delay addressing issues like school nutrition and mental health counselors mandated by a 2025 court order.

Even with recent amendments to the bill in what appeared to be response to public outcry, the legislation may have been too unpalatable, said Rep. Landon Brown, R-Cheyenne.

“To me, it looks as if there were too many downsides to the bill, that too many people saw too many downsides and therefore they were not even willing to try to fix it,” Brown said.

The vote doesn’t mean recalibration can’t still survive. A “mirror bill,” which is essentially an identical version, is still in play in the Senate with Senate File 81, “K-12 public school finance-2.” An individual lawmaker could also bring a new recalibration bill, though any fresh legislation would face the tight deadline and high voting standards of Wyoming’s budget session.

The bill’s demise is a mixed bag, according to Kim Amen, president of the Wyoming Education Association, the education lobby organization that brought the lawsuit that resulted in the 2025 court order.

“By not introducing the school funding recalibration bill in the House, it shows that lawmakers are hearing from educators, parents and community members who know the bill falls short,” Amen said in a Monday statement to WyoFile.

She is, however, troubled by the drafting of legislation like House Bill 123, which authorizes educational savings accounts. Similar legislation is currently tied up in a legal challenge.

“Instead of continuing to divert resources to private schools, lawmakers should focus on fully funding Wyoming’s public schools and the more than 93% of Wyoming students they serve,” Amen said.

### What happened

The recalibration process has unfolded in a series of meetings, studies and assessments that began last summer. It comes with extra gravity following a 2025 court decision that Wyoming has failed to adequately fund its public schools.

Working with consultants, the Legislature’s Select Committee on School Finance Recalibration drafted and refined a bill dictating how it will pay for public schools. The process has been scrutinized by educators and others who say Wyoming has failed to keep up with teacher salaries, leading to staff shortages and erosion of Wyoming’s historically stellar public school reputation. There was also vehement opposition to provisions to increase class sizes and mandate state health insurance participation, with superintendents arguing that the draft funding model would significantly reduce local control.

The bill was among a list of bills introduced to the House chamber on Monday. Because it’s a budget session, those bills required the aye vote of two-thirds of the lawmakers, or 42 votes. The recalibration bill failed by a single vote, with 41 in favor and 21 opposed.

The voting bloc that killed it included several Democrats and a handful of moderate Republicans. Brown, who has formerly served on the Legislature’s Education Committee, said he believed it was worth trying to improve the bill draft, particularly in light of the District Court’s 2025 decision and other previous court decisions that have delineated the process. Even if it would have been an uphill battle, as he expected.

“I do believe that we needed to show a good faith effort to the (courts) that we actually attempted to fix the issues they have found to be at issue with us, and the way we are implementing current statute,” Brown said.

Local school officials said a proposal advanced by Iowa lawmakers this week to increase state per-pupil funding by 1.75% is insufficient to support rising operational costs.

The Iowa Senate this week passed a [bill](https://www.legis.iowa.gov/legislation/BillBook?ga=91&ba=SF2201) setting the annual state funding increase at 1.75% for the 2026-2027 fiscal year. The measure now moves to the House for consideration.

The state’s funding formula for how much public school districts can spend on operations each year uses the district’s certified enrollment multiplied by a per-pupil amount, which is $7,988 this year and would be bumped up to $8,133 next year under the bill. The annual percentage increase in the per-pupil amount is referred to as supplemental state aid, or SSA.

A 1.75% increase would be the lowest level at which SSA has been set since fiscal year 2018-2019, when lawmakers approved an increase of 1%. The rate has been at or above 2% for each of the seven years since, peaking at 3% in fiscal year 2023-2024 before dropping again to 2% last year.

Local school leaders called the proposal inadequate to meet district needs.

“When the consumer price index is hovering around 3%, we are seeing that same rising cost with fuel, with food, with wages, salaries and benefits,” Western Dubuque Superintendent Dan Butler said. “There needs to be coverage to meet that ... or districts will need to dip into their (financial) reserves and their unspent balance, and continually doing that is not a long-term sustainable strategy.”

Butler referenced estimates from statewide officials that a 1.75% rate would lead at least 200 districts statewide to go on budget guarantee for the upcoming school year.

Because state code guarantees a school district 101% of its budget from the previous year, the budget guarantee system sees a district increase property taxes to hit that target if the SSA rate does not do so.

The current bill would give districts on budget guarantee state money in fiscal year 2027 to allow them to avoid increasing property taxes, but that money would be a one-time provision.

Dubuque Community Schools is operating on a budget guarantee this school year, and both Dubuque and Western Dubuque officials said they would likely be on a budget guarantee for the coming year if the 1.75% SSA rate is approved.

“Every time we don’t get the funds to meet those increases in our costs, then we’re going to have a gap, and over time, that gap has grown,” Dubuque Superintendent Amy Hawkins said. “I think our district has done a really good job of trying to be proactive and keeping our spending and our staffing within our means, but eventually, we get to a point where it’s really hard to find ways to reduce either our staffing or our resources that don’t end up having a huge effect on our (students).”

State Sen. Mike Zimmer, D-DeWitt, shared similar sentiments. Zimmer is a retired public school teacher and principal and former school board president and voted against the Senate bill.

“This is putting school boards in a precarious situation when they’re trying to find what I believe are appropriate salary and benefit increases,” he said. “School districts are going to have their backs against the wall.”

Zimmer, whose district includes Maquoketa and a portion of southwest Jackson County, noted that consequences will be particularly dire for school districts with declining enrollment. He argued that having fewer students does not necessarily allow schools to cut teachers, as the loss of one or two students per grade per year still requires the same amount of staff.

Sen. Carrie Koelker, R-Dyersville, pushed back on the idea that a 1.75% increase is insufficient funding for schools.

“The noise that we aren’t funding (schools) enough doesn’t back up the data we’re seeing: declining enrollment, but with higher administration costs that are outpacing increases in funding,” she said.

She argued that schools receive other federal and local sources of funding that bolster their per-pupil amount substantially and that the Legislature has appropriated more than $1.23 billion in new funding for kindergarten through 12th-grade education in Iowa since 2017.

“We’re delivering sustainable funding while increasing the focus on teaching the fundamentals Iowa students need to succeed,” she said.

Representatives in the Iowa House have not yet proposed an SSA rate for the coming year, but House Speaker Pat Grassley recently said Republicans in that chamber are considering an SSA proposal of 2% or higher, [as reported by the Iowa Capital Dispatch](https://iowacapitaldispatch.com/2026/02/10/iowa-senate-passes-1-75-increase-in-per-pupil-funding-for-schools). Iowa Gov. Kim Reynolds also suggested a 2% increase in her budget proposal, released Jan. 12.

That date means that today marks the Legislature’s self-imposed deadline to pass an SSA bill within 30 days of the release of the governor’s budget, though the Legislature has not met that deadline for several years.

local_articles.title: Letters to the editor: School funding still a problem; property taxes impact everyone
local_articles.summary: Letters to the editor: School funding still a problem; property taxes impact everyone  Dayton Daily News
local_articles.full_content:
### **Don’t forget there have been four DeRolph decisions**

Thank you for the column by Rob Scott on Sunday. I am a past president of the Urbana City School Board, and my wife taught first and second grades for 26 years in our public schools. Speaking from the perspective of one of only two non-republicans left in Champaign County, my wife being the other, it was so refreshing to hear from a Republican who remembers the (4) DeRolph decisions.

Most Republicans try to pretend they never happened, though often reminded by the tireless Bill Phillis.

You’re spot on that Ohio “…needs a state funding formula reflecting the true cost of education.”

That’s step one - basic. Unhappily, the Matt Huffmans of our state are hell bent to channel funds slated for public education to private, even parochial schools. In some cases this transfer is quite direct, making it nearly impossible for school districts to budget five years in advance as they are required to do.

This country was built on public education. Please pass the word among the Republican faithful.

**Dan and Cheryl Walter**

**Urbana**

### **Choices we make each day impact us**

As a physician, I was disappointed to see that the Cancer Society of Darke County held a fundraiser by raffling off red meat, a product linked to the development of several types of cancer.

While I support the Cancer Society’s efforts to help patients facing a daunting diagnosis, there are several other foods they could have chosen to raise money that would also promote good health and assist in recovery while undergoing cancer treatment.

In its annual cancer statistics report, the American Cancer Society predicted more than 2 million new cancer cases and more than 618,000 cancer deaths in the United States in 2025.

According to the Ohio Department of Health, neighboring Darke County alone has nearly 300 cases of invasive cancer diagnosed every year. Montgomery County averaged 3,326 cases last year.

Red and processed meat products are linked to cancer, according to a report from the World Health Organization published in Lancet Oncology. Researchers from around the world examined more than 800 studies looking at their cancer-causing properties.

Our own University of Dayton has committed to ensuring that more than 40% of its meals are plant based. Kettering Health’s “Food Is My Medicine” program advocates for a whole food, plant-predominant diet.

Scientific studies and my own personal experience show that the best way to improve the quality of your health and reduce the likelihood of developing cancer and other chronic diseases is to improve the quality of the foods you eat. Take advantage of free resources online and in our own community to get started today.

**Dr. Daniel M. Dawley**

**Findlay**

### **Eliminating property taxes bad for everyone**

In the Sunday, Feb. 8 Letters to the Editor, Timothy Cook of Centerville claims “The American dream of individual home ownership is in danger in Ohio and only Ohio’s citizens can rescue it.” He states that eliminating property taxes “...Is the only way to force the great re-set Ohio needs.”

He doesn’t seem to recognize owners of individual homes are not the only ones who pay property taxes. Renters pay them as part of their rent. Businesses pay them as part of the rental or ownership of their facilities. One way or another, we all pay property taxes.

Does he not realize that eliminating the property tax will not only cause school systems to be hurt, but also police, fire and EMS; they won’t have any funding. The result will be increased income or sales taxes or some other means of raising needed funds.

He has a very negative view of school boards. I wonder if he has no kids in school. so feels no responsibility to support them. I would love to hear his thoughts on that topic.

If it were up to me, Ohio’s Legislature would get off its collective duff and fix the school-financing system the Ohio Supreme Court adjudicated “unconstitutional” back in 1997. And, that they would re-institute the progressive tax system, to replace the flat tax that benefits only the rich and punishes the middle class and poor.

Eliminating the property tax will only hurt our kids and ourselves. It’s a really bad idea.

**Thomas Moon**

**West Carrollton**

local_articles.title: OPINION: Ohio’s lack of stable local school funding hurting taxpayers
local_articles.summary: OPINION: Ohio’s lack of stable local school funding hurting taxpayers  Dayton Daily News
local_articles.full_content:
Thank you Rob Scott for publicly calling out our state legislature in their exacerbation of the school funding problem. When Governor DeWine tried to address the school funding disparity issue by increasing the state’s contributions to lower-wealth districts in the 2021 Budget, with average per-pupil base funding increasing from $6,000 to $7,200, it seemed like we were headed in the right direction.

Now, with that plan seeing significant cuts with expenses based on 2022 costs and not current data, local school support from the state continues to decline relative to costs. When you add the recent reductions in effectiveness of the 20-mill floor to the declining relative state support, it has become increasingly difficult for school districts to plan accordingly.

An attempt to provide stable funding outside of property taxes has led to the trend of more urban school districts asking for income tax levies, which used to be relegated to rural communities.

For example, Fairfield City Schools has a slight operating surplus, yet is asking for a 1.25% permanent earned income tax levy, which they claim is the revenue equivalent of an 8.6 mill levy. Earned income taxes exempt retirement incomes and further shift tax burden onto working families who may or may not own property, rather than the taxing all real and utility property in the district. Based on property tax abstracts and existing TIFs, that is a $21.5m levy ask. 2026 General Fund Revenue expectations are $112m. In a normal budgetary climate, this would be an astoundingly large ask, especially for a levy without reduction factors. However, with mixed signals from the state and potential further property tax reductions, can we blame them?

If the funds were raised with 8.6 mils of property taxes, industrial/commercial property would pay 24.6% of the ask; not to mention the additional charge to large apartment complexes which typically only pass ~85% of the costs along through rent increases. Additionally, property tax levies are subject to reduction factors so the tax burden does not keep pace with inflation, whereas the income tax levy is charged uniformly as incomes rise.

The impetus for recent property tax reforms stem from a statewide outcry that seniors are being “taxed out of their homes” due to value increases. If a home increases 50% in value, but a property tax bill increases 15%, is that really a cause to defund local schools or have those districts push for earned income tax levies that just puts more pressure on young working families still saving to buy their first home?

Effectively, the state’s lack of stable local school funding is:

- Creating blind and over-reaching levy demand
- Incentivizing permanent income tax levies to avoid reduction factor
- Shifting tax burden away from large businesses and landlords
- Pushing tax burden toward working families

_Matthew Mesisklis is the City of Trenton finance director/tax administrator_

Washington’s Constitution is a lengthy read, but one thing it says clear as day is that it is state’s paramount duty to ensure sufficient funding for our public schools.

That means the state — not individual communities — is responsible for funding the basic education for every student.

Local levies were never meant to carry that weight. Levies exist so communities can invest in the programs and opportunities that reflect local values — things like outdoor education, music, arts and smaller class sizes.

In Bellingham, voters have consistently shown up for kids, time and time again. But today, levies aren’t paying for the extra.

They’re paying for heat, insurance, special education services and staff the state requires but doesn’t fully fund. This is a fundamental failure of the system.

Over the past several years, essential operating costs have skyrocketed. Utilities are up more than 40 percent. Insurance premiums have more than doubled. Special education needs have increased, to say nothing of the costs of hiring and retaining qualified educators in a region where housing costs continue to rise.

Meanwhile, state funding formulas have barely budged.

Instead of adjusting to real-world costs, the state continues to rely on outdated formulas that ignore local housing markets and staffing realities.

In Bellingham, this has resulted in a regionalization factor that is the lowest in the county despite having the highest median housing costs. It has also left special education deeply underfunded — to the point that the district must shift millions of dollars away from general education simply to comply with state law. Mandates that are rarely funded by the lawmakers who create them.

With enrollment fluctuating, the state has considered pulling back already-budgeted funds, even though every superintendent and school employee can tell you the cost of running a school does not shrink just because enrollment dips. You still have to heat the buildings.

You still have to run buses. You still have to provide mandated services. Fixed costs are still fixed.

This isn’t just unfair — it’s unconstitutional.

Student access to a stable, fully funded education shouldn’t depend on the generosity of their neighbors or the volatility of enrollment trends. When the state underfunds education, communities with stronger tax bases end up patching the holes, while less-resourced districts simply can’t. That inequity compounds year after year.

This month, the Bellingham Public School Board adopted a new resolution urging the legislature to fix these problems. Labor unions, community organizations and civic partners across the region are also signing onto a public letter calling on the state to honor its constitutional obligations. Both documents outline the same core issue: Washington cannot keep balancing its budget on the backs of local taxpayers and expecting communities to cover basic education costs.

Bellingham is fortunate to have a community that believes in its children. But it shouldn’t require extraordinary local effort just to give kids what our constitution has promised them for more than 130 years. It also shouldn’t leave other districts without the same resources struggling to provide the basics.

Fully funded public schools are not just an investment in children — they’re an investment in the stability and future of every community in Washington. Strong schools mean a strong workforce, a strong economy and a healthier, more resilient state.

This legislative session, lawmakers have a clear choice: continue with piecemeal fixes and outdated formulas or finally commit to a funding system that reflects the real cost of operating schools in 2026, not 2018.

Our community will keep doing its part.

_\-\- This commentary was written in tandem by the Bellingham Public Schools Board of Directors: Doug Benjamin, Camille Diaz Hackler, Jenn Mason, Scott Ritchey and Katie Rose_
